title: Volume of Rectangular Prisms
why: Volume of a rectangular prism is still length × width × height — but now the edges can be fractions or decimals. That means you need to multiply three numbers and handle simple fractions and decimals. Warm those feeder skills and the prism volume formula clicks.

learn.heading: Volume with fraction and decimal edges
learn.intro: The rule never changes: V = length × width × height. When an edge is a fraction or decimal, just multiply carefully. Half of a number means multiply by 1/2 (or 0.5).
examples: Decimal edge: 0.5 × 4 = 2, because 0.5 is half, and half of 4 is 2.
Fraction edge: 1/2 × 6 = 3. To multiply a fraction by a whole number, multiply the top: 6 ÷ 2 = 3.

tiers.intro: Multiply three whole numbers first, then meet a decimal.
Bring in a fraction or decimal edge.

items.q: C. A prism is 4 × 3 × 2 units. What is its volume? (cubic units)

tiers.intro: Stretch to volume with a fraction or decimal edge.

items.q: A. A prism is 6 × 2 × 0.5 units. What is its volume? (cubic units)
B. A prism is 1/2 × 4 × 5 units. What is its volume? (cubic units)

exit.q: 2. A prism is 5 × 2 × 3 units. What is its volume? (cubic units)
